transcript:
What you're watching is 800 tiny vertical marks trying to go quiet — starting as full white noise, then thinning out and dimming over about two and a half minutes, but never actually reaching black.

Here's the trick: in the "what persists" function, each mark carries two separate oscillations that never merge. One, called blood sway, physically shifts each mark left and right — that's the slow pulse, about an eleven-second period. The other, nerve tremor, doesn't move anything, it just controls brightness — a faster flicker, under three seconds. Even the decay math admits defeat: the comment says "35% amplitude persists at equilibrium — irreducible, not invisible." It's not fading to zero. It's fading to a floor.

So the piece never actually finds silence — it hits a mathematical baseline and hovers there, forever swaying and flickering. Cage sat in that soundless room and still heard his own blood and nerves. This is the same discovery, run as code: the artist tried to write "nothing," and 35% of something remained, on loop, permanently.
